title: Oxalates are ruining your health with Sally Norton

chunks.title: Introduction to Oxalates
chunks.summary: The podcast begins with a discussion about the common misconceptions surrounding meat and plant-based diets, emphasizing the role of oxalates in certain so-called 'superfoods'. The host introduces Sally K Norton, who has written a book called 'Toxic Superfoods'.
chunks.transcript: we've been told meats are toxic forever and in fact they're secretly the most nutritious food on the planet on this week's podcast I got to talk to my friend Sally K Norton about oxalates I love talking about oscillates and it was awesome to have Sally on the podcast and to talk to you about these she just wrote a book called toxic superfoods which is pretty appropriate because so many of the foods that we think of as healthy superfoods chard beet greens spinach are full of oxalates and they can accumulate in the human body and the kidneys and the joints and the soft tissues and the breasts and the bladder all sorts of places maybe even in the nervous system in the brain causing issues for humans a lot of times when I talk about plant defense chemicals people say what are you talking about that's just fallacy oxalates are not fallacy guys they are used as a plant defense chemical that plants use to protect themselves from fungi from organisms like insects potentially also from humans and to protect their seeds in the animal digestive tract and they're harmful for us and a lot of people get significantly better when they cut out oxalates or go very low oxalate in their diet my diet is not zero oxalate but the only real source of oxalates that I have in my diet is a little bit of fruit and I think my oxalate intake is less than 50 milligrams per day in the podcast we talk about limits and what you want to do in terms of oxalate consumption on a daily basis so this podcast is a gem if you don't know about oxalates you're about to have your mind blown enjoy this one with Sally K Norton and live and learn and share it with people in your life who may benefit because I think oxalates are as Sally says at the end of the podcast really basically a miracle cure for people it's such an easy thing to do it takes intention to know which foods are high versus low in oxalates but if oxalates

chunks.title: Understanding Oxalates
chunks.summary: Sally K Norton explains what oxalates are and how they are utilized by plants as a defense mechanism against predators. The talk delves into the health implications of oxalates accumulating in the human body, and the benefits some people experience when reducing oxalates in their diet.

chunks.title: The Health Risks of High Oxalate Foods
chunks.summary: The discussion transitions to specific high oxalate foods, such as spinach, chard, and beet greens. Sally shares concerns about the health risks these foods pose to vulnerable groups like infants, elderly, and those with chronic illnesses, highlighting misconceptions around their health benefits.

chunks.title: Oxalate and Kidney Health
chunks.summary: The conversation highlights the connection between oxalates and kidney health, discussing how dietary oxalates can contribute to kidney stones and other urinary tract issues. The importance of balancing oxalate intake with calcium is emphasized.

chunks.title: Symptoms of Oxalate Overload
chunks.summary: Sally outlines various symptoms associated with oxalate overload, including bladder issues, joint problems, and neurological symptoms. The potential for oxalates to cause widespread inflammation and interference with bodily functions is discussed.

chunks.title: Dietary Recommendations
chunks.summary: The podcast shifts focus to dietary recommendations, emphasizing the importance of reducing oxalate consumption and opting for lower oxalate foods while including nutrient-dense animal products like meat, liver, and dairy to support overall health.

chunks.title: Transitioning Off High Oxalate Foods
chunks.summary: Listeners are guided on how to transition from a high oxalate diet to a lower one, with warnings about potential withdrawal symptoms as the body adjusts. The importance of gradually reducing oxalate intake rather than making abrupt changes is stressed.
chunks.transcript: an adult who is eating lots of greens potatoes sweet potatoes we'll get into all the Oxley Foods even some high oxalate fruit and maybe they're getting vitamin C IVs and nothing's getting better and none of the doctors are even talking to them I mean this was not a diagnosis that I was ever taught in medical school the only context for the word oxalate in medical school is a calcium oxalate kidney stone which is the majority of kidney stones that humans get and I believe that the majority of calcium oxide kidney stones are related to dietary oxalate so they can be related to primary hyperoxylurea which is the sort of genetic predisposition to oxalate excretion and production that we talked about earlier but the majority of them are related to dietary oxalate again and even in the context of medical school when they're talking about calcium oxalate kidney stones there's never a real discussion that maybe you shouldn't eat a ton of spinach or beet greens or if you see a patient and they have kidney stones maybe you should ask them what they're eating that was never a conversation

chunks.title: Conclusion and Key Takeaways
chunks.summary: The episode concludes with a recap of the significant health implications tied to oxalate consumption and the benefits of educating oneself on dietary choices. Sally's book, 'Toxic Superfoods', is recommended for further understanding and guidance on the matter.
